content.text: When Claude decides to call a tool during a streamed response, the tool name and input arrive as a sequence of

events interleaved with any text output. This document shows how to handle those events correctly, emit live UI feedback during tool execution, and surface partial tool input to the user as it streams in.

attrs.alt: Streaming Tool Call Event Sequence — message_start, text deltas, tool_use block, execute, next turn

content.text: Parsing Tool Call Events from a Stream
The server must track multiple block buffers simultaneously — a single response can contain both a text block and one or more tool use blocks, each with its own index.
Claude can emit text, then a tool call, then more text. Use the block index to keep buffers separate.
events carry partial JSON fragments. Parsing mid-stream produces a
blocks in one response (parallel tool calls). The loop above handles arbitrarily many by keying buffers on
Streaming Tool Events to the UI
Users should see live feedback as each tool executes — not a frozen UI during the tool phase. Emit structured SSE events so the client can render a tool call indicator, then a result.
Consuming Tool Events in the Browser
The client must handle the new event types (
) to show the user what the agent is doing.
For tool calls where the input is long (e.g., a code writing tool), you can stream the partial JSON to the UI so the user sees the argument building up in real time.
useful for write-file or code-generation tools where the argument is large (hundreds of characters). Skip for small inputs like search queries where the partial is meaningless.
throttle the partial_input updates to at most once per 50ms to avoid flooding the DOM with micro-updates.
Never trust partial JSON for execution:
use partial input only for display. Always wait for
before passing the input to the actual tool function.
Claude can emit multiple tool use blocks in a single response when
is not restricted. Execute them concurrently with
and collect results before sending the next turn.
the Anthropic API requires that ALL tool results from a parallel call be returned in a single user message. Sending them in separate turns causes a
on tool results that failed so Claude can acknowledge the error and decide whether to retry or continue.
SSE connections drop due to network interruptions. The browser's
API reconnects automatically. For agentic streams, you need server-side logic to resume from where the stream was interrupted rather than restarting the full LLM call.

import asyncio, json
from redis.asyncio import Redis

async def resumable_stream(
    redis: Redis,
    session_id: str,
    messages: list[dict],
    tools: list[dict],
    last_event_id: str | None,  # from HTTP header Last-Event-ID
) -> AsyncIterator[str]:
    """
    If last_event_id is set, replay buffered events from Redis before
    opening a new LLM stream.
    """
    buffer_key = f"stream_buf:{session_id}"

    # Replay buffered events for reconnecting clients
    if last_event_id is not None:
        buffered = await redis.lrange(buffer_key, 0, -1)
        for i, raw_event in enumerate(buffered):
            event_id = f"{session_id}:{i}"
            if event_id == last_event_id:
                # Found the last seen event — replay from the next one
                for replay in buffered[i + 1:]:
                    yield replay.decode()
                break
        return  # Done replaying; client will reconnect for new events

    # Fresh stream — buffer events in Redis for reconnection
    event_idx = 0
    pipe = redis.pipeline()

    async for sse_event in full_agent_stream_events(messages, tools):
        event_id = f"{session_id}:{event_idx}"
        # Prepend event ID to enable Last-Event-ID tracking
        id_prefixed = f"id: {event_id}\n{sse_event}"
        yield id_prefixed

        # Buffer the event in Redis (keep last 200)
        pipe.rpush(buffer_key, id_prefixed)
        pipe.ltrim(buffer_key, -200, -1)
        pipe.expire(buffer_key, 300)  # 5-minute TTL
        await pipe.execute()
        event_idx += 1
browsers send this header automatically on reconnect (
) or you can set it manually with
by tracking the last received event ID.
200 events × ~200 bytes = ~40 KB per session buffer. For longer streams, use a larger buffer or store on S3.
is not found in the buffer (client was away too long), restart the full agent loop — the messages array provides full context.
Test the streaming parser in isolation using a list of synthetic events before wiring it to the live API.

import asyncio, json
import pytest

# Minimal async context manager for mocking the stream
class MockStream:
    def __init__(self, events):
        self._events = events

    async def __aenter__(self):
        return self

    async def __aexit__(self, *_):
        pass

    def __aiter__(self):
        return self._async_iter()

    async def _async_iter(self):
        for e in self._events:
            yield e

    async def get_final_message(self):
        # Return a minimal mock Message
        class FinalMsg:
            stop_reason = "tool_use"
            content = []
            class usage:
                input_tokens = 100
                output_tokens = 50
        return FinalMsg()

class FakeEvent:
    def __init__(self, type_, **kwargs):
        self.type = type_
        self.__dict__.update(kwargs)

class FakeDelta:
    def __init__(self, type_, **kwargs):
        self.type = type_
        self.__dict__.update(kwargs)

@pytest.mark.asyncio
async def test_streaming_tool_call_parser():
    tool_id = "toolu_123"
    events = [
        FakeEvent("content_block_start", index=0,
                  content_block=FakeEvent("text", type="text")),
        FakeEvent("content_block_delta", index=0,
                  delta=FakeDelta("text_delta", text="I will search for ")),
        FakeEvent("content_block_delta", index=0,
                  delta=FakeDelta("text_delta", text="that.")),
        FakeEvent("content_block_stop", index=0),
        FakeEvent("content_block_start", index=1,
                  content_block=FakeEvent("tool_use", type="tool_use",
                                          id=tool_id, name="web_search")),
        FakeEvent("content_block_delta", index=1,
                  delta=FakeDelta("input_json_delta", partial_json='{"query"')),
        FakeEvent("content_block_delta", index=1,
                  delta=FakeDelta("input_json_delta", partial_json=': "Python asyncio"}')),
        FakeEvent("content_block_stop", index=1),
        FakeEvent("message_stop"),
    ]

    text_blocks, tool_calls = await parse_stream(MockStream(events))

    assert text_blocks[0]["text"] == "I will search for that."
    assert len(tool_calls) == 1
    assert tool_calls[0]["name"] == "web_search"
    assert tool_calls[0]["input"] == {"query": "Python asyncio"}
    assert tool_calls[0]["id"] == tool_id
unit-test the event parsing logic with synthetic events before integration testing against the real API to get fast feedback on JSON buffer bugs.

Client receives events in batches, not real-time
Streaming tool calls requires tracking two types of content blocks simultaneously: text blocks (yielding
to the UI immediately) and tool use blocks (accumulating
— so the UI shows live feedback during the tool phase rather than a blank wait. Execute parallel tool calls with
and return all results in a single user message. For large tool inputs like code generation, optionally stream
preview events to the UI while accumulating the full JSON.
